Remark 1.14.

Suppose that \(f\in \cR(\alpha)\) on \([a, b]\) and \(a < c < b\text{.}\) Then \(\int_{a}^{c}1\, d \alpha=\alpha(c)-\alpha(a)\text{,}\) \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{a}^{c+ \frac 1k}1\, d \alpha=\alpha(c+0)-\alpha(a)\text{.}\) Since \([a, c]=\cap_{k}[a, c+ \frac 1k]\text{,}\) or one could think of \(\int_{a}^{c}1\, d \alpha\) as \(\int \chi_{[a, c]} (x)\, d\alpha\text{,}\) and since \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \chi_{[a, c+\frac 1k]} (x)=\chi_{[a, c]} (x)\text{,}\) one expects \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{a}^{c+ \frac 1k}1\, d \alpha= \int_{a}^{c}1\, d \alpha\text{.}\) This would require \(\alpha(c+0)=\alpha(c)\text{.}\) For this reason, one often chooses to work with an \(\alpha\) which is right-continuous.
Since \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{a}^{c- \frac 1k}1\, d \alpha=\alpha(c-0)-\alpha(a)\) and \([a, c)=\cup_{k}[a, c- \frac 1k]\text{,}\) it is reasonable to treat \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{a}^{c- \frac 1k}1\, d \alpha\) as \(\int_{[a, c)} 1\, d\alpha\) and \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{a}^{c+ \frac 1k}1\, d \alpha\) as \(\int_{[a, c]} 1\, d\alpha\text{;}\) namely, the notation of integration over a set is more precise than that of integration over a lower and upper limit.
in-context
